Grouped Summaries

Goal

This notebook shows how to compare groups after deciding what summary each group should receive.

It adapts the aggregation and iteration ideas from the clinical PRAMS examples.

Analytic Questions

We will ask:

  • How does mean systolic blood pressure differ by adult age group?
  • How do we separate the statistical summary from the visual display?
  • How does a dot plot compare with a bar chart for group means?

Load Data

nhanes_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"

nhanes <- read_csv(nhanes_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

Prepare Data

adult_bp <- nhanes |>
  filter(
    age_years >= 18,
    !is.na(age_group),
    !is.na(systolic_mean)
  )

bp_by_age <- adult_bp |>
  group_by(age_group) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_systolic = mean(systolic_mean),
    median_systolic = median(systolic_mean),
    sd_systolic = sd(systolic_mean),
    se_systolic = sd_systolic / sqrt(n),
    lower = mean_systolic - 1.96 * se_systolic,
    upper = mean_systolic + 1.96 * se_systolic,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(age_group = reorder(age_group, mean_systolic))

bp_by_age
# A tibble: 5 × 8
  age_group     n mean_systolic median_systolic sd_systolic se_systolic lower
  <fct>     <int>         <dbl>           <dbl>       <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
1 18-29       882          112.            111         11.5       0.387  111.
2 30-44      1298          115.            114.        13.0       0.361  114.
3 45-64      2005          124.            123.        17.1       0.381  124.
4 65-79      1618          129.            127         19.3       0.480  128.
5 80+         320          135.            134         22.0       1.23   133.
# ℹ 1 more variable: upper <dbl>

First Plot: Bars

Bars can show group means, but they can make a mean look like an amount that starts at zero.

p1 <- ggplot(bp_by_age, aes(x = age_group, y = mean_systolic)) +
  geom_col(fill = "gray75")

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Systolic Blood Pressure By Age Group",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Dot Plot With Intervals

For group means, points and intervals often focus attention on comparisons between groups.

p1 <- ggplot(bp_by_age, aes(x = age_group, y = mean_systolic)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.15, color = "gray50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue")

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Systolic Blood Pressure Increases Across Adult Age Groups",
    subtitle = "Points show means; intervals show approximate 95% intervals around the mean",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Interpretation: older age groups have higher mean systolic blood pressure in this sample. The intervals summarize uncertainty around each group mean, not the full spread of individual blood pressure values.

Check The Distribution Behind The Summary

Group means should not replace a distribution check.

p1 <- ggplot(adult_bp, aes(x = age_group, y = systolic_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "gray90", outlier.alpha = 0.3)

p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "The Individual Values Still Vary Within Each Age Group",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Takeaways

  • Decide what one plotted row should represent before plotting.
  • Manual summaries make the statistical step visible.
  • Dot-and-interval plots are often clearer than bars for comparing group means.
  • Summary plots should be checked against the distributions behind them.
